speaker: interviewer
text: Number two. I'm interviewing Henry Kronberg, in Las Vegas, Nevada, on December 26, 1995. And my name is Myra [? Slotnick. ?] Tell me what happened when your father arrived at prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I tell you that, coming back to the story that I was telling you, when my father was picked up by the Gestapo, that time by the office of the Gestapo and put on the truck, he didn't know actually where he was. Because he was-- I didn't have no way of communicating, and tell him that I'm trying to save his life or bring him over there to us. I had no-- no way of doing it. But I think maybe it's possible that the guy who told my father to go-- go on that particular truck, maybe he told them. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my father was under impression that he's going maybe to execution, especially when they picked up those kids. And he thought that he's going to be executed. And he-- he was also very much, but his life was saved. For-- for-- for-- not for long, unfortunately, but he was saved for-- for that time being. Now what was your question? He-- he-- I wanted to know the conditions under which you and your father lived in the prison, when you weren't working.

speaker: interviewee
text: The condition, in the beginning, was very bad because they didn't have a facility for 144 people. So-- so we were actually locked up in the cell. And we were sleeping on the floor. There was like 30, 40 people in one cell. But after a month, the condition improved because they designated a whole floor. They-- they confiscated a-- a-- a-- where nuns used to live. It was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: A convent.

speaker: interviewee
text: A convent. A convent. And that convent was right across the street from that prison. And they confiscated that convent, threw those nuns out. And they made a prison out of it. Because the prison was-- that original prison was too small. They did not have enough room. And they remodeled that to-- and made it to a prison. But we were fortunate that they designated a whole floor for the useful Jews. Our condition improved because we had our bunks over there. And we were not cramped anymore. And they gave us little better living condition. Because they needed us. So they tried to keep us in a better way. When my father came, from that-- after that ghetto was liquidated, my father came to prison. Unfortunately, he caught, in-- in-- in the ghetto, he caught typhoid fever. And when he came over to prison, he was already sick. I didn't know about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he got a high fever. And I tried to give him aspirin, but we didn't have any-- any medication, nothing. But we had aspirin. But the aspirin didn't help. And we had a doctor in the prison over there. And anybody who got sick had to report to the doctor. And my father, because the fever was so high, that we were afraid to report sickness because they didn't treat people. They used to shoot them. And my-- unfortunately, my father was so sick that he went to the doctor. And the doctor reported to the-- to the prison authority that he had the typhoid fever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told him that they're gonna take him to a hospital for-- to be treated. I stayed with my father early morning. I didn't go work that morning. And they took him on the truck. And I asked the-- the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] guys who took him on the truck, the jailers, where are you taking him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, oh, don't worry about it. We taking him to hospital. And we'll tell you which hospital we take him. And he will come back. That was the last time I saw my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A few late-- a few days later, I found out-- I'd seen that the guy, I had a bad omen. Because the-- the chauffeur that was driving the truck driver, who took him, was drunk. And when they had to take somebody on an execution or something, they used to drink a lot in order to get themselves courage or something. I don't know. And that was the last time I saw my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A few weeks later, through-- through somebody I found out that there was somebody brought from our prison to Plaszow to the field of Plaszow, and he was shot. And that was the last time I saw my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I met my wife? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The prison was on the-- on the third floor of that building. And every morning, when we went to work, we had to assemble in front of the-- of the-- of the building. And we saw all the other prisoners through the windows, through the-- through the bars. And there was-- all the prisoners, they used to keep on the first floor, second floor. The Jewish prisoners, they used to keep in the cellar. And there was a cellar. And the windows were bars up there. And we used to see all kind of different Jewish women prisoners. Because they kept women in that particular building where we were, in that monastery. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I see-- and I happened to see my-- a woman there. And later on, I found out that she was there. I didn't know who she was. And if you're going to interview my wife later on, you're going to find out that through her ability of being dress-maker, she was able to become a useful person, a useful Jew. Let's put it that way. Because they didn't treat us as a person. We were Jews. And they-- she came up to our floor. And they used her ability to become a dressmaker. And that's how-- because we were on one floor, in separate rooms. But the whole floor was open. The-- the-- the doors were locked, only on the other floor. But we had the freedom of walking between each room. Because there was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: So we could meet each other and see each other. So when we heard that another new person came up the floor, so naturally, everybody was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] interested to see who the person is. And-- not that we made a contact with each other, but we-- I heard that she was-- there was another person there. And that's all. And that's actually how we casually met. But right after the war, we were looking for some acquaintances, and that's how we got a little more closer with each other. How long did you stay in that prison, to do that painting job?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were coming closer to the border-- I mean, to the-- to our city. The war was not too well for the Germans. And they were liquidating the population of the prison. Where they were liquidating, they were sending into-- to concentration camps. At that time, we heard about the concentration camps. But we didn't know what extent it was and how bad it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometime in the summer of '44, the Russians were coming closer. And all of a sudden, the order came out to liquidate the population of the prison. And unfortunately, they had to liquidate half of our population too, of the-- of the workers. And they liquidated-- liquidated, that means, they took us away from the prison. And they send them to concentration camp. And later on, I found out that they went to Auschwitz from-- from the prison. We were then 140 people. And they took away about half of us, about 70 sent to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was fortunate that I was left there. We had two, three-- four painters, and I, because I spoke good German, so they made me the manager or the foreman of my-- of the paint shop. So because I was the foreman, they took all my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] helpers, I mean, all the people which I worked with, but they left me. And I stayed back. But they took all the other people. And the 70 people, they send them to concentration camp. And I was left. And we stayed there till January of '90-- '95.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 1945, the Russians were coming. But-- but they were very, very close to-- to the-- on the outskirt of the city. And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gave orders to take all the population from the prison and send us to-- send to concentration camp. We didn't know where we were going. They assembled us again. And they load us on trucks. And they load us to cattle cars, those famous cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that exactly for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They load us about 70 people in one car-- the-- box car, 70 people. We were like sardines in that box car. The box car was-- didn't have no window. Had two slots where actually air came in. And we were like sardines. We couldn't-- we were standing there. And occasionally somebody was able to sit down. So somebody else had to make room for. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for two days and two nights, we were on-- in that box car. It was a terrible condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sanitation in the box car?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any sanitation any place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once a day, they let us out from the box car, to-- on the side of the road, when the trains stopped. And we had guards on the top of those cars. And they let us out. But no sanitation. If you couldn't hold it, you had to make in your pants. It was terrible, terrible. And on the third day, they unloaded us, and we found that we were in Gross-Rosen, the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your entrance into Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was terrible. It was cold. It was bitterly cold. We had fairly good clothes. But they stripped us. They took all our shoes, everything that we had, all our belongings. And they shaved us. And they put us in a-- gave us a shirt and a jacket, prison jacket. And it was not adequate for the condition of the-- of the weather at that time. It was just terrible. That Gross-Rosen was actually a headquarters. Was-- and there was other labor camps all around it. And they liquidated all those labor camps, and they brought everybody to Gross-Rosen. There was a granite quarry. And maybe that camp was designated for 20,000 people. And all of a sudden we-- there was 150,000 people in that camp. We were stuck in a room like sardines. We were standing almost. We were-- were not on the outside, we're freezing. So they stuck-- in those barracks, the condition was terrible, was terrible. The food, they gave-- they gave us only one meal a day. And people were starving, starving there. Because they couldn't handle 150,000 people, when they had only facility maybe for 20,000. And people were dying like-- like-- like flies over there. And I stayed in that prison-- I mean in that camp-- for about three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a job in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any job. There were-- there was no-- occasionally they used us to do some manual labor, like carrying rocks. And we used to peel-- not potatoes-- we used to peel turnips because that's what they used to give us to eat. They used-- used to feed us with turnips, what they used to feed the animals with. So they used to feed us with that. So we used to peel the turnips. But actually, I didn't have any job, special, that was designated, because they had so many people. They didn't know what-- what to do with the people. Just like I said before, there was a quarry. And occasionally, when they had to fill a cavity, a hole, or something, they used to bring 10,000 people. And everybody picked up a few rocks. And that's how they filled in the-- in the holes. So that was the-- and it was bitterly cold. I tell you, I don't know how I survived through that, bitterly cold. And we were not dressed. We were shivering, not fed and shivering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after the three weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: After three weeks, they assembled us on a-- on a assembly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outside. And they gave everybody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a piece of bread and bowl of soup. And they loaded us in the box car, in the cars, on the train cars. But this time, not in a closed box cars. They loaded us on the open coal cars, where you loaded coal. And we didn't have no roof over our head. And they loaded on every-- on every car, they loaded maybe, 100 or 120 people on that car. The train, I understand, there was over-- which I was on, there was a long train. There was maybe 2,500 people or maybe more, maybe 3,000 people, on that particular train where-- where I was. And it was snowing. And there was-- I remember it very distinctly. It was Monday morning when they loaded us on that train. And we kept close to each other to-- to keep warm because it was snowing and rain. And we-- we didn't have nothing, no-- no roof over our head. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that took-- they never stopped the train. For seven days, they pushed us sometime on the side of the tracks. And we were standing for hours. And for seven days, without food or water, we came in. Somebody will tell me today that you can survive for seven days without food or water. We were actually drinking snow or rain, when it was snowing. So we used to [? plow them out ?] in order to. But from the 120 people on that car, half of us died. And the bodies were right on the side of the-- of the box car. So whoever lived, stayed on one side, and the bodies-- they-- they were dropping like flies, people. Maybe the reason I survived was because I was young. But older-- older people died on that train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was terrible, seven days. We came in to Dora. It was another concentration camp in Bavarian Alps. That's where we came in. Later on, I found that there was tunnels over there. And in those tunnels, they were making V-2 rockets. And that's where they brought us. What was a V-2 rocket?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you know the history, there was the V-2 rockets, where Germany, Hitler developed, and was shooting out, instead of having-- using airplanes bombing England. They were the first rockets. They were the first rockets developed by the German government. And they were shooting out from Peenemunde, I think, towards London. And that's where they were assembling those rockets, in that concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what month was that, and what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got off the train at Dora?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever was alive, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the snow was maybe three feet deep. But we got up from the-- from the train. And one thing about the Germans in the camps, whoever was still alive, they always took us to a showers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They rip-- they-- we had to discard our clothes, whatever we had on. And they put us-- and they took us to showers. We were starving. But they-- they washed us. And we took the hot showers. And they told us not to drink the water. Because-- that was one thing, I don't know why. But we were starving. We-- we didn't have what to eat. We didn't have what to drink. And they told us, don't drink the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: But look, people did drink the water. And they used to die after the-- after the water. I don't know why because they were dehydrated. And the water maybe didn't-- whatever it was, I listened. And I didn't drink the water from the showers, whatever it was. But they did give us showers. They stripped us naked. And after the showers, they opened up the door. And they said, go get your new clothes. I opened up the door, and I says, wait a second. Where should I go? There's-- there's snow. And the next building is 50 feet away. And I'm-- I'm stark naked. And they guy by the door, the guard, says, you go to the other building for-- for your clothes. I said what? He says, yes. And he kicked me out. And I had to run through the three feet of snow stark naked. To the other building for my-- for my clothes. How I survived, God knows only.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been, maybe, 25 degrees outside, or maybe 20 degrees. But maybe that saved us because we took that hot shower. That's what I-- and we ran so fast. And in that other building, they gave us some rags to-- to new clothes, and new wooden shoes. And that's where we got dressed.
